transcript.text: I just got into a fight at the thrift shop over a comment from a friend. My rent's due next week and I'm already behind, so I'm worried about how to pay. Since the bakery closed, I've been staring at my reflection and feeling like I'm not the person I used to be.

You're dealing with a lot right now, from the fight at the thrift shop to worrying about how to pay your rent, which is due soon. It sounds like these stressors are really getting to you. Can you tell me more about what's been going through your mind since the bakery closed and you've been noticing changes in how you see yourself?

I keep thinking about how I used to be the baker, but now I'm stuck juggling jobs and still can’t afford rent. That fight at the thrift shop made me feel like I'm losing my circle. My reflection is a constant reminder that I’m not the person I used to be.

You're feeling really disconnected from the person you used to be, and that's a tough spot to be in. It's like your reflection is holding up a mirror to all these changes you're experiencing, from your job to your friendships. Yeah, it makes sense that you'd feel like you're losing your circle after that fight at the thrift shop. What's been the hardest part for you about adjusting to these changes?

Honestly, the hardest part is that I feel like I'm losing the people I used to share the shop with and now I can't even pay rent, so I'm stuck in this loop. The fight at the thrift shop made me feel like I'm losing my circle, and the rent is due next week. I keep staring at my reflection and feeling like I'm not the person I used to be.

The fight at the thrift shop really brought home how much you're struggling to hold on to the relationships that matter to you, and now with rent due next week, it's like everything is piling up. It's not just about the financial stress, but about feeling like you're losing the people and the life you used to know. Yeah, it makes sense that staring at your reflection would be a tough reminder of all these changes. Can you tell me more about what you think would help you feel more connected to the person you used to be and the people you care about?

I think if I could get back to baking, even a little batch, it would remind me of who I was. But the rent is still looming and I don't have the money to buy ingredients. I keep staring at myself and wondering if I look like the baker I used to be, and I'm not even sure if the friend I fought with will still want to hang out again.

It's really tough to be in a situation where you're struggling to pay rent and feeling like you're losing touch with the things that bring you joy, like baking. You mentioned that getting back to baking, even just a little, could help you feel more connected to who you used to be. That makes sense, given how much baking meant to you in the past. What do you think would be the first step in making that happen, even with the rent looming and not having the money for ingredients right now?

I’d try to find a discount store or ask a friend for some flour. The rent’s due next week and I’m not sure I can afford a small batch. I keep looking in the mirror and wondering if I even look like the baker I used to be.

You're really torn between trying to make ends meet and holding on to the things that bring you joy, like baking. It's like you're being pulled in two different directions, and it's hard to know which way to turn. On one hand, you're exploring ways to afford a small batch of baking ingredients, which could be a step towards reconnecting with your past identity as a baker. On the other hand, the rent is due soon, and you're not sure if you can afford it. Can you tell me more about what you think would happen if you did find a way to bake, even just a little bit?

If I could bake, I’d at least feel a little normal again, but I don’t even know if I can afford flour or if I’ll be able to pay rent afterward. I keep looking at my reflection and feeling the same gap between who I was and who I am now, and I’m scared the friend I fought with won’t want to hang out again.

You're feeling really overwhelmed by the thought of not being able to afford flour or pay rent afterward, and on top of that, you're scared that your friend won't want to hang out again after your fight. It's like you're seeing a gap between who you used to be as a baker and who you are now, and that's really tough to deal with. If baking could bring you a sense of normalcy, what do you think is the smallest step you could take towards making that happen, even if it's just a tiny batch?

I guess the smallest step would be to see if I could just use the flour I have left from that last batch and maybe borrow a few more from a friend who knows I’m in a pinch. But the rent’s still due next week and I’m not even sure I can afford a single loaf. Every time I look in the mirror I keep thinking I’m not the baker I used to be, and that makes me feel even more out of place. I wonder if I can actually pull this off before the landlord calls.

You're considering using the flour you have left and maybe borrowing some from a friend to make a small batch, which could be a step towards taking action and feeling more connected to your past identity as a baker. At the same time, you're really unsure if you can afford even a single loaf, and the rent being due next week is weighing heavily on you. It's like you're being pulled in two different directions, and it's hard to know which way to turn. What do you think would be the most important thing to focus on right now - finding a way to make this small batch of bread, or addressing the rent and financial stress?

I think the rent has to come first, but I keep staring at my reflection and feeling like I’m not the baker I used to be. I’m also worried the friend I fought with will ditch me, which just adds to the pressure. It’s hard to decide whether to focus on paying rent or trying to fix my image and relationships.

The rent being due next week is really weighing on you, and on top of that, you're feeling this huge disconnect from the person you used to be as a baker. It's like you're being pulled in two different directions, and it's hard to know which way to turn. You're also worried about your friend ditching you, which just adds to the pressure. It sounds like you're trying to prioritize the rent, but this feeling of not being the same person is really sticking with you. Can you tell me more about what you think would happen if you could find a way to make some progress on the rent, even if it's just a small step?

I think if I can just chip in some of the rent, maybe I’ll feel less like I’m drowning. Still, every time I look in the mirror I just see a stranger, and that makes me worry the friend I fought with will cut me off. I’m stuck on whether to focus on the money or the image.

You're feeling really overwhelmed by the thought of paying rent and dealing with the emotional aftermath of your fight with your friend, and on top of that, you're struggling to connect with the person you used to be as a baker. It sounds like just chipping in some of the rent might give you a sense of relief, but at the same time, you're worried about your friend cutting you off and feeling like a stranger to yourself. It's like you're being pulled in two different directions, and it's hard to know which way to turn. Yeah, it's tough to decide whether to focus on the money or the image, but maybe taking a small step towards addressing the rent could help you feel more grounded and able to tackle the other issues.

I’ve got to get the rent sorted first, but I keep staring at my reflection and feeling like I’m not the baker I used to be. The friend I fought with might still be mad, and that just adds to the pressure.

You're really feeling the pressure to get the rent sorted, and at the same time, you're struggling to connect with the person you used to be as a baker. It's like you're being pulled in two different directions, and it's hard to know which way to turn. Yeah, it can be really tough to deal with financial stress and feelings of disconnection from your past self. It sounds like just getting the rent sorted might give you a sense of relief, but at the same time, you're worried about your friend being mad at you and feeling like a stranger to yourself. What do you think would be the smallest step you could take to start addressing the rent, even if it's just a tiny bit?
